summary: DHH argues that the hand-wringing over AI-generated content ('slop') ignores the fact that humans have been producing equally terrible content ('sludge') for years. He points to SEO-driven content mills, clickbait, and conversion-optimized garbage as proof that human-made content was already degraded. He contends that letting AI handle this soul-crushing work is actually more humane than forcing people to produce it. The post concludes with a fatalistic observation that most people won't opt out of consuming low-quality content regardless of whether humans or machines produce it.
key_points: Human-produced SEO content, clickbait, and conversion-optimized writing is just as bad as AI slop — and more degrading because it wastes human potential
Letting machines produce low-quality content is more humane than forcing humans to do soul-crushing creative drudgery
The demand for endless low-quality content isn't going away — people's screen time and consumption habits guarantee a market for it
The option to disengage from low-quality content has always existed, but most people won't take it
money_quotes: It's an affront to humanity to make sentient beings do this work.
Turning human potential, creativity, and ingenuity into content sludge is a process no more dignified than turning pink slime into chicken nuggets.
Let the little robots barf up tokens to unlock the next basis point of incremental conversion. Better them than us, I say.
This is exactly the soul-crushing, creative drudgery that machines were made to munch through without complaint.
But couldn't we do without sludge or slop, you say? Sure, right after we reach a shared state of nirvana.
So stop whining about the AI slop. You're already steeped in human sludge.
The door to exit both was always there. But you're not going to open it, are you?
key_insight: AI slop is a more humane replacement for the soul-crushing content drudgery humans were already doing, and the people complaining about it were never going to stop consuming garbage content anyway.
